theory.concept:
Pivoting turns row values into columns — instead of one row per category, you get one row per group with a column for each category.

- SQLite has no built-in PIVOT keyword, so it's done manually with CASE inside an aggregate function
- The pattern: SUM(CASE WHEN category_column = 'X' THEN value_column ELSE 0 END) AS X, repeated for each category you want as a column
- Usually combined with GROUP BY on whatever should remain as rows
theory.syntax:
SELECT group_column,
  SUM(CASE WHEN category_column = 'A' THEN value_column ELSE 0 END) AS A,
  SUM(CASE WHEN category_column = 'B' THEN value_column ELSE 0 END) AS B
FROM table_name
GROUP BY group_column;

example.query:
-- 1. Pivoting total order amount by category, per customer
SELECT customer,
  SUM(CASE WHEN category = 'Electronics' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS electronics,
  SUM(CASE WHEN category = 'Clothing' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS clothing,
  SUM(CASE WHEN category = 'Grocery' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS grocery
FROM Orders
GROUP BY customer;

shared_table.name: Orders
shared_table.create_sql:
CREATE TABLE Orders (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
  customer TEXT,
  order_date TEXT,
  amount INTEGER,
  category TEXT
);
shared_table.insert_sql:
INSERT INTO Orders (id, customer, order_date, amount, category) VALUES
(1, 'Aditya', '2024-01-15', 2500, 'Electronics'),
(2, 'Priya', '2024-02-20', 1800, 'Clothing'),

(6, 'Anjali', '2024-03-22', 2700, 'Grocery'),
(7, 'Vikram', '2024-01-05', 1900, 'Grocery'),
(8, 'Priya', '2024-03-10', 1200, 'Grocery');

questions.prompt: For each order month, pivot total order amount into columns for 'Electronics', 'Clothing', and 'Grocery'.
Pivot the count of orders per category into columns, per customer, only for customers with more than one order.
Pivot total amount spent into columns for just 'Electronics' and 'Grocery', per customer.
